title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardianship of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Te Tiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds deep cultural, historical and ecological importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its waters have sustained our communities for generations.
learningObjectives: Analyze historical and contemporary human impacts on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using primary source evidence
Explain the concept of kaitiakitanga and evaluate its application to local waterway management
Collaboratively design and present a culturally-informed restoration proposal for a section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Articulate personal connections to local waterways and commitments to ongoing kaitiakitanga practices

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa | Connecting to the Waterway
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku for a guided orientation with local kaumātua, who will share pūrākau (traditional stories), original place names, and historical significance of the awa. Students create observational sketches and reflective journals about their personal connections to water.

activities.title: He Tātari Taiao | Environmental Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits and ecological survey methods, students assess the current health of the awa (testing pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate diversity). They compare their findings with historical data and identify potential sources of environmental pressure.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o te Kāinga | Local Stories and Perspectives
activities.description: Students conduct interviews with local farmers, tangata whenua, council environmental officers, and long-term residents to gather diverse perspectives on the awa's past, present challenges, and hopes for its future. They analyze these interviews for common themes and tensions.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Māori | Māori Environmental Principles
activities.description: Through workshop with a local kaitiaki, students explore key Māori concepts including kaitiakitanga (guardianship), mauri (life force), whakapapa (genealogical connections), and rahui (protection mechanisms) and discuss how these principles can guide sustainable management.

activities.title: Te Mahere Whakaora | The Restoration Plan
activities.description: In small groups, students design a detailed, evidence-based restoration proposal for a specific section of the awa. Proposals must integrate scientific data, mātauranga Māori, community perspectives, and practical actions, and are presented to a panel including kaumātua and local council representatives.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council: 'State of the Environment Report' and freshwater quality data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Local kaumātua and representatives from Mana Whenua o Ngāti Kahungunu
Historical photographs and land use maps of the Mangakōtukutuku catchment from the MTG Hawke's Bay Tai Ahuriri
Water testing kits, digital microscopes, and iPads for field data collection and documentation

tasks: Group presentation of a detailed restoration proposal for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Individual written reflection connecting the project to personal values and commitments to kaitiakitanga

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies L5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Social Studies L5: Understand how cultural interactions impact on cultures and societies
Social Studies L5: Understand how people participate in community action
